words: SAM SCHRAGER: This is Axel Anderson interview number four.
AXEL ANDERSON: Yeah, in '50, 1950. I put in two days and nights on that
and I never went to bed. There was a crew at Camp 36 up there on the
Palouse, oh five-six miles above Laird's camp, y'know, up there in the
pocket. So we had that crew on it— No, wait a minute— it was— oh
no, not in '50, it was during the war days — they had twelve hundred
sailors on it— from the camp out at Coeur d'Alene. What'd they call
that?

speaker: SS
words: I don't know the name of that one.

speaker: SS
words: LAURA SCHRAGER: Was that Fort Wright?

speaker: SS
words: (Note:- Camp Farragut- F.R.)

speaker: AA
words: No. No, that's here in Spokane. What the hell did they call that camp

speaker: AA
words: now? A Been several times since. Yeah, they had twelve hundred sailors

speaker: AA
words: there, and they couldn't do anything. The captain was sitting down in

speaker: AA
words: the valley there at the store down in the meadows, and they had a walkiewind

speaker: AA
words: talkie, y'know you could talk to them, so if the come up, and flared

speaker: AA
words: up a little bit they had to retreat, he called 'em back, and what little

speaker: AA
words: they had done in the day, y'know they lost that and it cooled off a lit

speaker: AA
words: tle bit and they go back and make a little more trail, and of course they

speaker: AA
words: get another wind puff come and away they go again, but they didn't get

speaker: AA
words: anything done. We got in there with a couple- three dozers and we had it

speaker: AA
words: corralled in there, after noon with that little crew, we had about seventy

speaker: AA
words: oh seventy-eighty men and we had four dozers; we corralled it in a little

speaker: AA
words: bit, then we back-fired.

speaker: LS
words: Which fire was this?

speaker: AA
words: They called it the Saunders fire.

speaker: SS
words: You can still see it when you're up—

speaker: AA
words: Oh yes, it burnt that whole sidehill, it burnt, y'know.

speaker: SS
words: It burned a big area.

speaker: AA
words: It burnt a hell of a big area.

speaker: AA
words: Well, maybe it was the '40's. Could have been '44 then maybe. It was

speaker: AA
words: during the war then anyway.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know how that fire started?

speaker: AA
words: I guess lightning, I suppose.

speaker: SS
words: When you came back after World War I, when you came back to the woods,

speaker: SS
words: did you start in as walking boss right away?

speaker: AA
words: No, I run camp there for— No, by God, yes, I started in camp

speaker: AA
words: for seven months, in the spring, early summer. No, I got the walking boss

speaker: AA
words: after the Spokane Fair, in September. I come back in March y'see.

speaker: LS
words: What did that mean when you became walking boss?

speaker: AA
words: Well you go— I got charge of everything that belonged to the woods dep

speaker: AA
words: artment, y'see. I was assistant superintendent, I had a man over me, and

speaker: AA
words: I was roadmaster, I looked after all the railroad tracks, and trainmaster,

speaker: AA
words: I looked after all the trains, and whatever have you— I also went from

speaker: AA
words: one camp to the other. I had to go to the camp— I saw each one at least

speaker: AA
words: once a week — first one camp and then the other. But then I had all

speaker: AA
words: kinds of duties between that. They always needed something.

speaker: LS
words: Was that just for the Elk River area?

speaker: AA
words: For the Elk River mill then, y'see, the mill was running there then. That

speaker: AA
words: run up to 1930- the last. To December the 22nd, 1930. That' the end of it,

speaker: AA
words: And they started in 1911. They built it in '10, started the next year,

speaker: AA
words: They built it, I guess, for twenty year: operation. that, oh, I don't

speaker: AA
words: know there have been timber there yet, they could

speaker: AA
words: have had that mill running all the time and still running if they wanted

speaker: AA
words: to. They're still logging there. And hauling the timber over here to

speaker: AA
words: Newport, I guess most of it goes to Newport and Cusick, down the river.

speaker: AA
words: The Diamond National still logs up there and haul it over. And Potlatch,

speaker: AA
words: too, I think, all past Elk River and down to Potlatch.

speaker: SS
words: Well was your job to make sure that the foremen were doing the right work,

speaker: SS
words: or was it to help the foremen out?

speaker: AA
words: Help the foreman, if I could, yeah. And we all had to work together—

speaker: AA
words: all had to work together, see, just like any other team. We had the railroad

speaker: AA
words: to get the logs away from 'em— well if we didn't work together and

speaker: AA
words: have a train there and get the logs loaded and take 'em away from 'em,

speaker: AA
words: the foreman couldn't do nothing. We all had to work together as a team.

speaker: AA
words: And you got to do the same thing now.(Sam— how many buzz saws did you

speaker: AA
words: borrow or rent to be sure you had the proper sound effects?—) And for

speaker: AA
words: supply and so forth, y'know, you had to more or less, had to see that

speaker: AA
words: the roads were open and equipment— to bring it in to them. They were

speaker: AA
words: miles away from noplace. So every one— we each one needed the other.

speaker: AA
words: I needed their help and they needed mine. From the mill out.

speaker: LS
words: Would you go back home then every night? Or would you—?

speaker: AA
words: Yeah, I did at Elk River, I usually come home about ten-eleven o'clock

speaker: AA
words: at night. I left at five in the morning.

speaker: SS
words: Did you sleep with your boots on, too? (chuckles)

speaker: AA
words: Well, most of the time I took my shoes off. But a lot of times that's

speaker: AA
words: the only thing I had off from Sunday morning — Sunday night till Sat

speaker: AA
words: urday evening, the only thing I took off was the shoes and the hat!

speaker: AA
words: Laid on top of the bunk in the camp somewheres a couple of hours.

speaker: AA
words: After we got the trucks, y'know, one summer, there we had, that was up

speaker: AA
words: on the at Mason Meadow, we had eighteen log trucks and four

speaker: AA
words: trucks, two loading crews the double crew, three loading crews

speaker: AA
words: in the woods there at the camp. Each one had six trucks to load; twenty

speaker: AA
words: mile haul down to the railroad head, and double crew then too- so there

speaker: AA
words: was six loading crews every day in the woods and two more at the rail

speaker: AA
words: road, load 'em on flatcars. And they went to work at four o'clock in the

speaker: AA
words: Up morning and the first crew worked eight hours to noon, then the other

speaker: AA
words: crew come and took over and loaded till eight o'clock at night; sixteen

speaker: AA
words: hours. And I was on my feet all the way, and then after that they were

speaker: AA
words: gone and done with that, y'know, you see that was running smooth all day,

speaker: AA
words: then I'd go down and see how the train crews getting along and how the

speaker: AA
words: railroad track was—there was fifty miles of railroad from there in to

speaker: AA
words: Bovill that we hauled every day,on the rails. Had two camp one

speaker: AA
words: up in the woods, and one at the rail head. Six section crews on the

speaker: AA
words: road between there and Bovill, so I had to be able to— in the morning

speaker: AA
words: when I got out I had to be able to stand there at the camp and look from

speaker: AA
words: there to Bovill fifty mile and see where the crews was. I had a phone

speaker: AA
words: along the road, but that was all, so I could talk to 'em, so they could

speaker: AA
words: talk to me too, once in a while. The train crew had to use it y'know,

speaker: AA
words: 'course they was two or three train crews along the road there and we

speaker: AA
words: had to keep them apart so they wouldn't run into each other. Well, fun,

speaker: AA
words: a lot of fun. Kept me busy, I was busy from morning to night.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like a lot of work.

speaker: AA
words: The day was never long enough. The day was always too short and it's

speaker: AA
words: only twenty-four hours. So most of the time I worked for twenty-four

speaker: AA
words: hours, or a lot of times.

speaker: SS
words: Would you get— would you walk that whole length on a lot of those days?

speaker: AA
words: Oh I had a little spedthere on the rail most of the time that I used.

speaker: AA
words: Little bitty one that I could pick upland set it off, and I knew when the

speaker: AA
words: train whistle— I heard them coming or see them coming, I'd pick it off

speaker: AA
words: set it on the track and they go and I'd go the other way. Till I met

speaker: AA
words: another one. I could go forty miles an hour on the rails, y'know, didn't

speaker: AA
words: take long to go— about an hour I could go from one end of— to the otherfifty

speaker: AA
words: miles. Take little over an hour if I had a clear track, didn't

speaker: AA
words: get off the track.

speaker: SS
words: Is that all one camp.

speaker: AA
words: We had one camp there.

speaker: SS
words: Which camp was it?

speaker: SS
words: Well, let me see—

speaker: AA
words: Thirty Four, I think they called it, Thirty Two and Thirty Four, one on

speaker: AA
words: that summer

speaker: AA
words: the end of the rail and one on the other And

speaker: AA
words: that's the only one we had for Potlatch mill. They only run one shift we

speaker: AA
words: got thirty-two carloads every day.

speaker: SS
words: About how much wood would that be?

speaker: AA
words: Oh that was, oh, anyway from two hundred and twenty to two hundred and

speaker: AA
words: forty thousand feet, the logs scaled, quarter of a million, pretty near a

speaker: AA
words: dayA. Two shifts, y'know, then I used to have to get between fifty and

speaker: AA
words: sixty carloads, when they run double shift. Then we had five, six log

speaker: AA
words: ging camps, but after the Depression it cut down. They had more— they

speaker: AA
words: didn't waste so much in the mill, they got more particular after that,

speaker: AA
words: saved the lumber more. They got more board feet out of the logs than

speaker: AA
words: they did before.  Utilized the timber better, saved more of it. They

speaker: AA
words: even saved the sawdust, now they save the bark too.

speaker: LS
words: Did they use to burn an area after they logged it?

speaker: AA
words: Oh,yes, you bet they burned it. You betcha, set fire to it every spring

speaker: AA
words: and burnt it. Stay away from the hazard in the summer when it got dry.

speaker: AA
words: Yes, they used to burn that every spring, burn trie slashing. Then later

speaker: AA
words: years when they piled it then burned it in the fall, piled the brush.

speaker: LS
words: But before that they just burned—

speaker: AA
words: Just broadcast it, yes, let go.

speaker: LS
words: Did it get away often?

speaker: AA
words: Ohhhh- not too much, no. No, we took the whole crew and they'd usually

speaker: AA
words: just had it trenched off and the slashing was pretty much in one area, so

speaker: AA
words: you could trench it first before you set fire, they set from the outside

speaker: AA
words: and work in. Have it all trenched up, y'know, start fire— the way, if

speaker: AA
words: the wind was from the north, y'know, burn a good strip on the south side

speaker: AA
words: first, then go over on the north side and set it and boy in an hour and

speaker: AA
words: she was (Chuckle) But they had protection then, they had

speaker: AA
words: it burnt out so the spark wouldn't fly, and then a big crew there to

speaker: AA
words: watch it if anything should happen to come across, y'know, they were there

speaker: AA
words: to stop, get it out. Oh, yes, sure burnt lot of slashing there. Sections

speaker: AA
words: of it every spring. Just as soon as the snow get off,'Joff the

speaker: AA
words: ground, you know, then it was dry enough. Some evening when you thought

speaker: AA
words: it might rain that night, set fire to it. (Chuckles)

speaker: LS
words: Did they then reseed it all?

speaker: AA
words: Most of it seeds itself, naturally. That ground is full of seeds, you

speaker: AA
words: know, so if a quick fire goes over that gives the seed a chance to come

speaker: AA
words: up again. 'Course, if it's like in the summer when a fire goes out it

speaker: AA
words: burns too deep, it burns all the— that needles and moss and stuff, you

speaker: AA
words: know where the seed is laying, that burns that up and burns too deep, you

speaker: AA
words: know there's no seed left. But like we burnt in the spring, y'know, a

speaker: AA
words: couple of years then it was green like a wheat field. Sometimes, you

speaker: AA
words: know they come so damn thick, you know, when you step there you probably

speaker: AA
words: stepped on couple a dozen young seedlings. They were just like a mat

speaker: AA
words: come up. Then it dies out itself, them years. Now I see they thin it

speaker: AA
words: out, prune the trees and thin out. That'll make 'em grow a lot faster.

speaker: AA
words: In the early years, they didn't do that. You know some places you could

speaker: AA
words: see along the road where they been in that— they pruned the

speaker: AA
words: trees, you know, up as high as they can reach, and it look like a park in

speaker: AA
words: there, you can see a long ways. Haven't you noticed that? Along the

speaker: AA
words: Aroad when you go up in the upper country there? It look pretty nice

speaker: AA
words: toward Clarkia and on in that way. I see they do that quite a bit, or

speaker: AA
words: they used to do it, when they had the CCC in there they done a lot of

speaker: AA
words: that on around Clarkia on towards Bovill, and that look pretty nice. And

speaker: AA
words: I see they do itAmore than they ever did.

speaker: LS
words: Did you have much to do with the Forest Service in that area?

speaker: AA
words: No. Juat a few fires and so forth, that was all. Only I worked with them

speaker: AA
words: during the three C outfits. I was six months up there on the Pend'Orielle

speaker: AA
words: for

speaker: AA
words: River and six months up on the St. Joe up at Herrick, worked,xthem a year

speaker: AA
words: there, during the hard years, in '33. '33 it was, yeah. '33 and '34.

speaker: LS
words: When you were working around Elk River, they didn't ever complain about—?

speaker: AA
words: No. There was not too much forest land in there, that was state and they

speaker: AA
words: had the State fire patrol, y'know, in there, Potlatch. Potlatch Forest,

speaker: AA
words: Potlatch Protective. They got their office in Orofino, one in Orofino,

speaker: AA
words: and they used to have a station at Elk River, too. They had their own

speaker: AA
words: fire department, you know, Potlatch has. And he is also a State Fire

speaker: AA
words: Warden. So that take care of the state. Oh, the Forest Service come in

speaker: AA
words: there a little bit, not too much. They come in there with the blister

speaker: AA
words: rust, and had a few camps in there, but we never had too much set with

speaker: AA
words: the Forest Service until that first fire I was on was on that

speaker: AA
words: Saunders fire, that was a Forest Service fire, and they come and got us.

speaker: SS
words: So they used to be there but they didn't have very much to do with most

speaker: SS
words: of the woods around the country.

speaker: AA
words: Only up when we logged up there at Avery. Half of that— some of that

speaker: AA
words: was NP land, P land, you know when they built the roads, they got every

speaker: AA
words: other section; Forest Service had one and NP had the other. That what

speaker: AA
words: we logged there for several years, they're still logging part of it, I

speaker: AA
words: think now. Have been for the last thirty years. Then when they logged-

speaker: AA
words: I don't remember now— the NP had number one section and the Forest Ser

speaker: AA
words: vice the even two. See- so like the NP had one, three, five, seven, and

speaker: AA
words: Forest Service had two, four, six, and so on. And that whole damn country

speaker: AA
words: there was- oh, I don:t know, there was hundreds of sections, all them

speaker: AA
words: mountains belonged to the NP and the Forest Service. Now, I see they got— when you

speaker: AA
words: go in on the Forest Service land, you know,

speaker: AA
words: you gotta build the road according to where the Forest Service want 'em.

speaker: AA
words: They stake 'em out for you, and you gotta build them to their specifica

speaker: AA
words: tions, I guess. When I was there, you know, you just went in and built

speaker: AA
words: 'em.

speaker: AA
words: Well, did you log on land that the Forest Service owned, or was it—?

speaker: AA
words: Oh, yeah. Yes.

speaker: AA
words: It was Forest Service owned that you logged on?

speaker: AA
words: Yes, yes, and tb?y paid their; the stumpage, paid that to the government,

speaker: AA
words: whatever they paid for I don't know. And the same the other owned by

speaker: AA
words: the NP Railroad,h they scaled it and paid them so much stumpage, what-

speaker: AA
words: ever that was. (Sam, I left out the "dog-talk"!!)

speaker: LS
words: Did you have very much going on when you,started as walking boss? It seems

speaker: LS
words: like a big jump to have taken to, you know, being in charge, like that.

speaker: AA
words: That was. When you started out you didn't know what the hell you were

speaker: AA
words: looking for, didn't know what you'd run into before the day was out.

speaker: AA
words: When you started out in the morning you had to be able to— when I worked

speaker: AA
words: around Bovill I had six camps and a steelgang, that was the seventh.

speaker: AA
words: And I'd six train crews and a loading crew with each one of them, and

speaker: AA
words: they had a section crew at each camp. That was my job, y'see, to look

speaker: AA
words: after all them train crews, after all them loading crews, look after

speaker: AA
words: all the railroad; I had section crews, one at each camp. That was part

speaker: AA
words: of it. And then in the meantime go and look up the camp boss and see

speaker: AA
words: what he needed and wanted. I started out Monday morning and usually I

speaker: AA
words: come back to Bovill Saturday afternoon unless something broke down and

speaker: AA
words: I had to go to the shop, you know, and that was usually during the night.

speaker: AA
words: We had a shop out of Bovill and every Sunday we brought in two or threeusually

speaker: AA
words: two loading rigs and two locomotives or three, every Sunday,

speaker: AA
words: and rotated them back and forth, y'know, so every Sunday at the shop, so

speaker: AA
words: Sunday was just as busy as any other day, see that they got fixed up for

speaker: AA
words: the week. See them locomotives, we had to wash 'em— that water is not too

speaker: AA
words: ever good- we got the water for themout of the creek where ,we could siphon it

speaker: AA
words: up in the tank, mud and lots of alkali in it. They coated, the boilers

speaker: AA
words: get coated on the inside and you had to wash that out about every two

speaker: AA
words: weeks,had to wash 'em. Clean them boilers. And then towards the end

speaker: AA
words: of two weeks, you know, they start foaming. They got too much mud in

speaker: AA
words: side of that, you see, and that won't work very good. Just like soap,

speaker: AA
words: and they coat, you had to get that off, inside, have 'em clean. So I

speaker: AA
words: had seven days a week and that many nights, never missed, they all come!

speaker: LS
words: Is that when you started out?

speaker: AA
words: Oh, I done camp for a long time. I done camp, oh, for ten years there.

speaker: AA
words: I started in with the crew in '11, 'course part of the time I was in

speaker: AA
words: France, pretty near years out of that, '17, '18 and '19— part of '19.

speaker: SS
words: Were there many guys, Axel, that were working seven days and seven nights

speaker: SS
words: a week?

speaker: AA
words: Oh, everybody that was on the monthly payroll. You got paid by the month

speaker: AA
words: you know, and you were sold, you were sold to the company. You were on

speaker: AA
words: twenty-four hour duty and they expect you to look after what you were

speaker: AA
words: supposed to do. And a lot of it, you know, the things broke down and

speaker: AA
words: you know you had to get it fixed. Break down in the afternoon or some

speaker: AA
words: thing you had to get it fixed and get out and go to work in the morning

speaker: AA
words: again. Somebody had to look after it. And the one in charge of it, he

speaker: AA
words: was usually the one that was hooked- because they paid the crews that

speaker: AA
words: were working under him. They were paid by the day you know, or night,

speaker: AA
words: whatever when you call 'em out, you know, they were paid. But the monthly

speaker: AA
words: man he was the one that was hooked. (Chuckles) There was no day off for—

speaker: AA
words: no vacation time and no overtime, you were paid by the month, so much a

speaker: AA
words: month and that was it. Now, you know, I think— well I think there was a

speaker: AA
words: half a dozen of them — it would take a half a dozen of 'em now to do

speaker: AA
words: what I did, almost. What I did forty years ago or fifty. Now, I see,

speaker: AA
words: you know they had— they finally just before I left working they had a

speaker: AA
words: foreman for every eight, ten or twelve men and I had two hundred— three

speaker: AA
words: hundred. (Laughs) Yeah. There were twice there around camp, you know,

speaker: AA
words: only had one camp, 'course then I had a lot of assistants, I had

speaker: AA
words: three assistants and had a cat boss. All around.

speaker: AA
words: We had a eleven man track crew work nights, fix up cats and dozers and

speaker: AA
words: locomotives and loaders,whatever we had on the rail that was moving.

speaker: AA
words: And they had to be serviced at night, greased and fueled and cleaned up

speaker: AA
words: and if they were broke down,parts to them and fix 'em up so they go in

speaker: AA
words: the morning again. Cookhouse; that was a big item right there to feed—

speaker: AA
words: there were three hundred and fifty men in one camp— there was a little

speaker: AA
words: town. (Chuckles) We had hot water— running water in the bunkcars and

speaker: AA
words: bathhouse, kitchen— and that take a lot of food. That kitchen stove

speaker: AA
words: was never - never cooled off from spring to the next one— the year

speaker: AA
words: around that stove was hot. Took lot of wood.

speaker: SS
words: What did you get paid a month, when you started as walking boss?

speaker: AA
words: Two hundred and fifty dollars a month. See wages didn't come up till

speaker: AA
words: along about the time I quit, that's when the wages come up, after '50.

speaker: AA
words: I guess the last there when I quit, I think I got six hundred and board,

speaker: AA
words: see. They didn't charge me 'cause I was eating in every place and I

speaker: AA
words: had a charge accout so if I was in town, you know, I just turn it in

speaker: AA
words: wherever I stayed,' in the hotel, 'cause I couldn't always get to where

speaker: AA
words: I wanted to go so I stop in Maries or Moscow, Orofino, or Coeur

speaker: AA
words: d'Alene, wherever I was, you know, go to the hotel and at the end of the

speaker: AA
words: month turn it in for meals and lodging. Meals and lodging and carfare

speaker: AA
words: if I used the train. A little book mark down each day whatever I spent,

speaker: AA
words: at the end of the month, turn it in, and I get my money back.

speaker: AA
words: Now, I

speaker: AA
words: imagine,probably pay fifteen-hundred a month, eighteen, three or four

speaker: AA
words: times as much as when I was working. And it's all days now. And they

speaker: AA
words: have so many assistants-now the logging superintendent over there at

speaker: AA
words: Bovill he got eight or ten walking around doing nothing just under him.

speaker: AA
words: And the same with the camp foreman, you know, he's the foreman in the

speaker: AA
words: camp and he's got- for every nine men he an assistant. Two unions

speaker: AA
words: and all of that stuff. I think that's overdone. Everybody thinks they're

speaker: AA
words: doing so damn much, you know, I can hear people now, them young fellas

speaker: AA
words: you know, they're so tired, and they're sitting around driving a car

speaker: AA
words: all day— they're so tired. I never got tired until I was hart so bad

speaker: AA
words: that I couldn't hardly walk and I was still working. But, you know then

speaker: AA
words: I knew what, tired was. After I got hurt there— so later

speaker: AA
words: years there I knew what it is to get tired. But until after I got up

speaker: AA
words: in the sixties I never felt tired, got a cup of coffee and a doughnut

speaker: AA
words: and I could go another eight hours. Nothing to it, no sleep or anything

speaker: AA
words: that didn't bother me. Now the young fellas, now they get so Goddamn

speaker: AA
words: tired, they say if they driveAfour or five hours along the road sitting

speaker: AA
words: in the car, "Oh, By God, I'm just all in, I get so tired!" (Laughs)

speaker: AA
words: I can drive from here to Frisco and I don't get tired yet, that won't

speaker: AA
words: hurt me any. Only that I got to slow down now in the evening. I don't

speaker: AA
words: want to be out on the road after dark. That bothers mefotherwise that

speaker: AA
words: wouldn't bother me. I'm going down there next month, I'm going to take

speaker: AA
